content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

November 3, 1983

Harold C. Stehle
1520-69 East Capitol Expressway
San Jose, CA 95121

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our No. A-83-240

Dear Mr. Stehle:

This letter is sent in reply to your request for advice
concerning possible conflicts of interest on the part of
San Jose City Councilmembers in voting on rent control and other
matters.

Although I am unable to provide you with specific
conclusions in the situations you raised without additional
facts and a request for advice from the councilmembers involved,
I hope the following discussion is helpful to you. I have also
enclosed a copy of the Commission pamphlet on the disqualifi-
cation provisions of the Political Reform Act. Please note
the section on the last page of the pamphlet concerning the
filing of complaints.

The Political Reform Act prohibits a public official from
making, or participating in the making of, a governmental
decision in which he or she has a financial interest.
Government Code Section 87100. An official has a financial
interest in a decision when it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect on an
interest in real property belonging to the official worth $1,000
or more, on a source of income to the official of $250 or more
within the 12 months preceding the decision, on a business
entity in which the official has an investment of $1,000 or
more, or on a business entity for which the official is an
employee, officer, director, partner, or trustee. Government
Code Section 87103. Accordingly, city councilmembers must not
vote or participate in matters which could materially affect
their interests in real estate, for example, or a business
client from whom they have received $250 or more in income.

Harold C. Stehle
November 3, 1983
Page 2


     Please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-6444 if I can
provide you with additional information or our Enforcement
Division at (916) 322-6441 if you would like to file a complaint
concerning a specific situation.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Diane Mahra Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DMF:plh
Enclosure

Harold C. Stehle
1520-69 East Capitol Expressway
San Jose, California 95127

Oct. 19, 1983

Fair Political Practices Comm.
P.O.Box 807
Sacramento, Calif. 95804

Please advise me if a member of our City Council,who owns a Hotel
which has a number of long time residents, must abstain on voting
on an issue concerning Mobile Home Tenants. Where is the conflict?

The tenants of Mobile Homes in San Jose have twice,before overflow-
Council Chambers,lost the chance to amend our Rent Stabilization
Ordinance to correct a loophole that almost voided the intent of the
Ordinance. With a vote of 5 to 4 we are being frustrated as they say
we need a majority of the Council,not of these voting. In this way
it members are able to stop any action of the Council, We understand
that interest in income property calls for abstaining; but in this
case only a clause that does not effect other rentals is the issue.

This seems to be a very unfair situation for the tenants who must
wait a year to try to make a change by elections. It is a dangerous
situation for the City also as many times in other cases where the
City may want to make changes or improvements they can be stopped
by these same 4 votes.

One of those voting in favor of the Landlords is a prominent member
of a Stock Market Firm. Undoubtably some of the Landlords may be
customers of his,or at least his firm. Doesn't this represent a
possible conflict of interest ? If so, he should also abstain, but
we still would like to know if the Hotel owner must abstain in this
particular case.

I represent a large number of tenants who are anxious to know the
answer to this, so we would appreciate an answer as soon as convenient
to you. Thank you.

Sincerely yours,

Harold C. Stehle

Phone: 408-238-1976

P.S. I am sorry, I should have mentioned our Council including the
Mayor has 11 members.

H.S.

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662                322-5660              322-5901              322-6441              322-6444


October 24, 1983


Harold Stehle
1520 - 69 East Capitol Expressway
San Jose, CA 95121
Re: A-83-240

Dear Mr. Stehle:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Diane Fishburn, an attorney
in the Legal Division of the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about your advice
request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief, Legal Division

BAM:plh

sections.question: Must a member of San Jose City Council who owns a motel and is a landlord of long-term residents abstain from voting on an issue concerning Mobile Home Tenants? Where is the conflict? The question specifically asks whether a councilmember with interests in income property, including a landlord stake in mobile home park tenancies affected by rent control ordinance amendments, must recuse themselves from voting.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether San Jose City councilmembers with financial interests in rental properties (mobile home parks, motels, income property) must disqualify themselves from voting on rent control ordinance amendments affecting those properties.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC cannot provide specific conclusions without additional facts and direct requests for advice from the councilmembers themselves. However, the Political Reform Act prohibits public officials from voting on decisions in which they have a financial interest, including real property worth $1,000+ or income sources of $250+ from the prior 12 months. City councilmembers must abstain from voting on matters that could materially affect their rental property interests.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is a complete advice letter response with incoming request letter attached. OCR quality is moderate with some text degradation but core content is readable. FPPC response dated November 3, 1983 provides general legal framework and analysis referencing Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103, though declines to render specific advice. Incoming request from October 19, 1983 is included with degraded OCR but captures the essential question regarding conflict of interest and disqualification.

embedding.summary: Harold Stehle requested FPPC advice regarding whether San Jose City councilmembers with financial interests in mobile home parks and rental properties must recuse themselves from voting on rent control ordinance amendments. FPPC responded that officials cannot participate in governmental decisions with material financial effects on their real property interests exceeding $1,000 or income sources exceeding $250, but declined to provide specific conclusions without additional facts and direct councilmember requests.
